derivation: Gmail is a finished, vendor-managed application consumed over the browser; the user manages no runtime or infrastructure, which is the definition of Software as a Service. IaaS = raw VMs, PaaS = runtime for your own code, Private Cloud is a deployment model not a service model.

TCP/IP expands to Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol; IP addresses and routes packets, TCP segments, reliably delivers and reassembles them. B, C, D are invented expansions. Minor quibble: 'secure' is loose wording (TCP is reliable, not secure), but only A has the correct expansion so it is unambiguous.

A hub is a layer-1 repeater with no MAC table, so a frame on one port is flooded out all other ports (one collision domain). Router, bridge and gateway all make forwarding decisions on addresses, so they do not broadcast to every port.
Hybrid cloud is a deployment model: private cloud / on-premise combined with public cloud. A and C mix service models (SaaS/PaaS/IaaS) with deployment models; D is the opposite of hybrid.
Multi-cloud = consuming services from two or more distinct cloud providers (e.g. AWS + Azure). A is the trap: many services from one provider is still single-cloud.
AWS, Azure and Google Cloud are cloud platforms. Oracle Database is a licensed DBMS product, not a cloud provider (Oracle's cloud arm is OCI). The 'NOT' item therefore picks D.

P2P has no dedicated server: every node can both request and provide services, i.e. act as client or server. A describes client-server; C (wireless) and D (wide area) are irrelevant to the architecture definition.
Pay-as-you-go bills by measured consumption (compute hours, GB, requests); no charge for idle capacity. Fixed monthly (A) and lifetime subscription (D) are usage-independent, B is free.

Security and protection of data and resources is a standard stated goal of computer networks. A, B and D are written as anti-goals (complicate usage, limit sharing, decrease efficiency) and eliminate on sight.

PaaS supplies runtime, middleware, DB and deploy tooling so developers ship code without managing OS/servers, which is the model targeted at application developers. IaaS leaves OS/stack management to them; SaaS is a finished app; DaaS is desktop/data as a service.
A PAN connects one person's own devices over a few metres (Bluetooth, IR, USB). A = WAN/MAN, C = LAN/enterprise network, D = VPN.

Worst case QuickSort: pivot is always the extreme element, so partition splits into sizes 0 and n-1, giving T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + O(n) = T(n-1) + O(n); unrolling sums 1..n = n(n+1)/2 = O(n^2). C is the best-case/balanced recurrence, D is the 1:9 split (also n log n), A is not a QuickSort split.

Inner loop runs i times while i halves each outer iteration: n + n/2 + n/4 + ... + 1 = 2n - 1. Compiled and ran the function: count/n converges to 2.0000 (n=10^6 -> count=1999993), so work is linear, O(n).
Worst cases: Merge O(n log n), Bubble O(n^2), Quick O(n^2), Selection O(n^2). Lowest worst case is Merge Sort.

All-pairs shortest paths is solved by Floyd-Warshall, which builds the solution from optimal subproblems over intermediate vertices, i.e. dynamic programming. Greedy is Dijkstra (single source), and backtracking / divide-and-conquer are not used for APSP.
